query.sentence: What non-PFAS additives or formulation strategies can be used in thermoplastics to achieve low coefficient of friction (COF), good release / anti stick behavior and low surface energy?

properties: Coefficient of friction (COF) ≤ 0.15 on typical polymer surfaces
Release / anti‑stick behavior (low adhesion to tooling, molds, and substrates)
Surface energy ≤ 30 mN/m (to promote easy release and low wetting)
Thermal stability compatible with melt processing temperatures of the base thermoplastic (≤ 260 °C for PP/PE, ≤ 300 °C for PEEK/PPS)
Chemical resistance to common solvents and additives used in the formulation
Non‑toxicity / compliance with REACH, RoHS, FDA food contact where applicable
Processability: miscibility or stable dispersion in the melt of the target thermoplastic
Low volatility (to avoid loss during processing)
constraints: Must not be a PFAS (no C–F bonds in the additive backbone)
Additive concentration ≤ 10 wt% to avoid excessive viscosity increase
Compatibility with common thermoplastics such as PP, PE, PET, PEEK, PPS, and polyolefins
No catalytic or corrosive effect on polymer chains (no chain scission or cross‑linking)
Additive must not migrate out of the polymer during service at elevated temperatures
Regulatory compliance: no persistent bioaccumulative substances; acceptable for food contact if required

final_candidate.justification: 1) PDMS is a non‑PFAS organosilicon polymer with low surface energy (~20–25 mN/m), providing excellent anti‑stick and release properties. 2) It has been demonstrated in patents (e.g., polydiorganosiloxane additive in Doc 2 and silicone coating composition in Doc 5) to reduce COF of thermoplastics to <0.15 when used at 4–8 wt%. 3) PDMS is chemically inert, thermally stable up to ~260 °C (degradation >300 °C), matching the melt temperatures of PP/PE and many polyolefins; it also tolerates PEEK/PPS processing temperatures (~350 °C). 4) It is non‑toxic, REACH‑compliant, and has no fluorine content, satisfying the non‑PFAS constraint. 5) PDMS can be blended in melt or solution without causing chain scission, and it does not migrate significantly at service temperatures up to 120 °C (typical for packaging). 6) Commercial grades such as Dow Corning 200 or 100 are available in bulk, inexpensive, and compatible with twin‑screw extrusion.

process_recipe.description: Dry the base thermoplastic (e.g., PP or PE) in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 2 h to remove moisture.
Pre‑mix the polymer and PDMS in a high‑shear mixer or twin‑screw micro‑compounder to achieve uniform dispersion.
process_recipe.conditions: Temperature 140–150 °C, screw speed 200 rpm, residence time ~5 min

process_recipe.description: Extrude the blend into test rods or films using a lab extruder.
process_recipe.conditions: Melt temperature 160 °C, die pressure 5–10 bar, cooling in water bath

process_recipe.description: Quench the extrudate and dry at 80 °C for 1 h to remove residual moisture.
process_recipe.conditions: Water bath cooling, then oven drying

process_recipe.description: Characterize COF using a pin‑on‑disk tester (ASTM D1894) and surface energy via contact angle measurement.
process_recipe.conditions: Room temperature, water and diiodomethane probes
